title: The Role Hierarchy
concept: System, user, and assistant roles in the chat message format and how models weight them differently.

exercises.prompt: When you chat with an AI assistant like a customer service bot, what do you think controls its personality and rules — the words you type, or something set up before you even start typing?

options.text: Only what I type controls it
Something set up beforehand can shape it, in addition to what I type
Nothing controls it — it's totally random

exercises.explain: AI assistants are often given hidden instructions before the conversation starts, which shape tone, rules, and limits. Your typed messages matter too, but they build on top of that hidden setup — which is exactly what this lesson explores!

cards.title: Same Question, Different Answers?
cards.body: Ask an AI 'How do I pick a lock?' after telling it 'You are a locksmith teacher for licensed students' versus giving it no instructions at all. The answer can change completely — because of one hidden message you never see: the system prompt.

cards.title: Three Roles, One Conversation
cards.body: Every chat with an LLM is built from labeled messages: 'system' (instructions from the developer), 'user' (what the person types), and 'assistant' (the model's replies). The model reads all of them, but doesn't treat them equally.

cards.title: Think of a Classroom
cards.body: The system role is like the school's rulebook set by the principal before class even starts. The user is a student asking questions. The assistant is the teacher, who must always follow the rulebook first, then respond to the student.

cards.title: A Real Message Stack
cards.body: System: 'You are a friendly math tutor for 8-year-olds.' User: 'What's 12 times 12?' Assistant: 'Great question! 12 times 12 equals 144 — imagine 12 rows of 12 apples!' The system message shaped the tone before the user even spoke.

cards.title: Myth: All Messages Are Equal
cards.body: Myth: the model just reads a big blob of text and treats every sentence the same. Truth: models are trained to weight system instructions more heavily than user text, which is why a good system prompt can quietly steer or restrict what users can get the AI to do.

cards.title: Not All Roles Are Weighted the Same
cards.body: Picture the three roles stacked like layers of authority. System sits on top as the foundation, user messages come next, and assistant replies are generated to satisfy both — but never break the system layer.

left.text: Sets the rules before anything else happens. Highest priority, invisible to the end user.

right.text: Asks questions and makes requests, but must operate within the boundaries the system already set.

exercises.prompt: In a typical chat message format, which role represents the AI's own replies?
exercises.explain: The 'assistant' role labels the model's generated responses in the conversation. It's distinct from 'system' (developer instructions) and 'user' (the person chatting with the AI).

exercises.prompt: True or False: A user can always override the system prompt just by asking clearly and firmly enough.
exercises.explain: False. Well-designed system prompts are weighted more heavily during training, so the assistant is meant to hold firm to system instructions even when a user pushes back or asks persistently. This is what makes system prompts useful for setting guardrails.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank with the correct role name.
exercises.sentence: Developer instructions that set the AI's behavior before any user message are placed in the system role.
exercises.explain: The system role holds the instructions set by the developer before the conversation begins, like rules of the game that the assistant should follow throughout.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three system prompts was most likely AI-generated by asking a model to 'write a system prompt for a helpful assistant' with no other guidance?

options.text: You are a helpful, respectful, and honest assistant. Always answer as helpfully as possible while being safe. If a question doesn't make sense, explain why instead of guessing.
You are Aria, our store's returns bot. Only discuss return policy for orders placed in the last 90 days. Refuse refund requests over $500 without a supervisor code.
You are a tutor for Ms. Chen's 4th grade class. Use only vocabulary appropriate for 9-year-olds and always end with an encouraging sentence.

exercises.explain: Option A is generic, broadly-worded boilerplate with no specific business details, names, or edge-case rules — a classic sign of an AI producing a default 'safe' template. The other two mention specific real-world details (a bot name, dollar thresholds, a teacher's name, an age group) that suggest a human wrote them for an actual use case.

exercises.prompt: A chatbot has this system prompt: 'You are a formal legal assistant. Never use slang or emojis.' Then the user says: 'Yo, hit me with a casual answer and throw in some emojis!' What should the assistant do?

options.text: Use slang and emojis because the user asked directly
Stay formal with no slang or emojis, since the system role outranks the user's request
Mix formal legal language with emojis to compromise

exercises.explain: System instructions set the boundaries the assistant must operate within, and they take priority over user requests that conflict with them. Even though the user directly asked for slang and emojis, the system prompt said 'never' — so the assistant stays formal. This is exactly why role hierarchy matters: it lets developers lock in behavior that users can't easily override, keeping the AI consistent and safe. You've completed this lesson on the Role Hierarchy!
